author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm XXXIX

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The title of this Psalm, which we have just chanted and proposed to discuss, is, “On the end, for Idithun, a Psalm for David himself.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Here then we must look for, and must attend to, the words of a certain person who is called Idithun; and if each one of ourselves may be Idithun, in that which he sings he recognises himself, and hears himself speak.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For thou mayest see who was called Idithun, according to the ancient descent of man; let us, however, understand what this name is translated, and seek to comprehend the Truth in the translation of the word.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: According therefore to what we have been able to discover by enquiry in those names which have been translated from the Hebrew tongue into the Latin, by those who study the sacred writings, Idithun being translated is “over-leaping them.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Who then is this person “over-leaping them”? or who those whom he hath “over-leaped”?…For there are some persons, yet clinging to the earth, yet bowed down to the ground, yet setting their hearts on what is below, yet placing their hopes in things that pass away, whom he who is called “over-leaping them” hath “over-leaped.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: You know that some of the Psalms are entitled, “Songs of Degrees;” and in the Greek it is obvious enough what the word ‡ναβαθμῶν means.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For ‡ναβαθμοὶ are degrees (or steps) of them that ascend, not of them that descend.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Latin, not being able to express it strictly, expresses it by the general term; and in that it called them “steps,” left it undetermined, whether they were “steps” of persons ascending or descending.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But because there is no “speech or language where their voices are not heard among them,” the earlier language explains the one which comes after it: and what was ambiguous in one is made certain in another.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Just then as there the singer is some one who is “ascending,” so here is it some one who is “over-leaping.”…Let this Idithun come still to us, let him “over-leap” those whose delight is in things below, and take delight in these things, and let him rejoice in the Word of the Lord; in the delight of the law of the Most High.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I said, I will take heed to my ways, that I sin not with my tongue” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 1).…For it is not without reason that the tongue is set in a moist place, but because it is so prone to slip.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Perceiving therefore how hard it was for a man to be under the necessity of speaking, and not to say something that he will wish unsaid, and filled with disgust at these sins, he seeks to avoid the like.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To this difficulty is he exposed who is seeking to “leap beyond.”…Although I have “leaped beyond” the pleasures of earth, although the fleeting passions for things temporal ensnare me not, though now I despise these things below, and am rising up to better things than these, yet in these very better things the satisfaction of knowledge in the sight of God is enough for me.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Of what use is it for me to speak what is to be laid hold of, and to give a handle to cavillers?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore, “I said, I will take heed to my ways, that I sin not with my tongue.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I keep my mouth with a bridle.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore is this?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is it on account of the religious, the thoughtful, the faithful, the holy ones?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These persons hear in such a manner, as to praise what they approve; but as for what they disapprove, perhaps, among much that they praise they rather excuse than cavil at it; on account of what persons then dost thou “take heed to thy ways,” and place a guard on thy lips “that thou mayest not sin with thy tongue”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hear: it is, “While the wicked standeth over against me.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is not “by me” that he takes up his station, but “against me.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why?…Even the Lord Himself says, “I have yet many things to say unto you, but ye cannot bear them now.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And the Apostle, “I could not speak unto you as unto spiritual, but as unto carnal.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Yet not as to persons to be despaired of, but as to those who still required to be nourished.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he goes on to say, “As babes in Christ, I have fed you with milk, and not with meat; for hitherto ye were not able.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Well, tell it unto us even now.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Neither yet now are ye able.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Be not therefore impatient to hear that which as yet thou art not capable of; but grow that thou mayest be “able to bear it.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is thus we address the little one, who yet requires to be fed with kindly milk in the bosom of Mother Church, and to be rendered meet for the “strong meat” of the Lord’s Table.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what can I say even of that kind to the sinner, who “taketh his stand against me,” who either thinks or pretends himself capable of what he “cannot bear;” so that when I say anything unto him, and he has failed to comprehend it, he should not suppose that it was not he that had failed to comprehend, but I who had broken down.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore because of this sinner, who “taketh up his stand against me, I keep my mouth as it were with a bridle.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I became deaf, and was humbled, I held my peace from good” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this person, who is “leaping beyond,” suffers some difficulty in a certain stage to which he hath already attained; and he desires to advance beyond, even from thence, to avoid this difficulty.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I was afraid of committing a sin; so that I spoke not; that I imposed on myself the necessity of silence: for I had spoken thus, “I will take heed to my ways, that I may not sin with my tongue.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whilst I was too much afraid of saying anything wrong, I kept silence from all that is good.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For whence could I say good things, except that I heard them?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “It is Thou that shalt make me to hear of joy and gladness.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And the “friend of the bridegroom standeth and heareth Him, and rejoiceth on account of the bridegroom’s voice,” not his own.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That he may speak true things, he hears what he is to say.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is he that “speaketh a lie,” that “speaketh of his own.”…When therefore I had “put a bridle,” as it were, “on my lips;” and constrained myself to silence, because I saw that everywhere speech was dangerous, then, says he, that came to pass upon me, which I did not wish, “I became deaf, and was humbled;” not humbled myself, but was humbled; “and I held my peace even from good.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whilst afraid of saying any evil, I began to refrain from speaking what is good: and I condemned my determination; for “I was holding my peace even from what is good.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And my sorrow was stirred up again” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Inasmuch as I had found in silence a kind of respite from a certain “sorrow,” that had been inflicted upon me by those who cavilled at my words, and found fault with me: and that sorrow that was caused by the cavillers, had ceased indeed; but when “I held my peace even from good, my sorrow was stirred up again.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I began to be more grieved at having refrained from saying what I ought to have said, than I had before been grieved by having said what I ought not.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And my sorrow was stirred up again.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And while I was musing, the fire burned” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 3).…I reflected on the words of my Lord, “Thou wicked and slothful servant, thou oughtest to have put My money to the exchangers, and I at My coming should receive it again with usury.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And that which follows may God avert from those who are His stewards!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Bind him hand and foot, and let him be cast into outer darkness; the servant, who was not a waster of his master’s goods, so as to destroy them, but was slothful in laying them out to improve them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What ought they to expect, who have wasted them in luxury, if they are condemned who through slothfulness have kept them?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “As I was musing, the fire burned.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And as he was in this state of wavering suspense, between speaking and holding his peace, between those who are prepared to cavil and those who are anxious to be instructed,…in this state of suspense, he prays for a better place, a place different from this his present stewardship, in which man is in such difficulty and in such danger, and sighing after a certain “end,” when he was not to be subject to these things, when the Lord is to say to the faithful dispenser, “Enter thou into the joy of thy Lord,” he says, “Then spake I with my tongue.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In this fluctuation, in the midst of these dangers and these difficulties, because, that in consequence of the abundance of offences “the love of many is waxing cold,” although the law of the Lord inspires delight, in this fluctuation then, (I say), “then spake I with my tongue.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To whom? not to the hearer whom I would fain instruct; but to Him who heareth and taketh heed also, by whom I would fain be instructed myself.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I spake with my tongue” to Him, from whom I inwardly hear whatever I hear that is good or true.—What saidst thou?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Lord, make me to know mine end” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For some things I have passed by already; and I have arrived at a certain point, and that to which I have arrived is better than that from which I have advanced to this; but yet there remains a point, which has to be left behind.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For we are not to remain here, where there are trials, offences, where we have to bear with persons who listen to us and cavil at us.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Make me to know mine end;” the end, from which I am still removed, not the course which is already before me.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The “end” he speaks of, is that which the Apostle fixed his eye upon, in his course; and made confession of his own infirmity, perceiving in himself a different state of things from that which he looked for elsewhere.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he says, “Not that I have already attained, or am already perfect.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Brethren, I count not myself to have apprehended.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And that you might not say, “If the Apostle hath not apprehended, have I apprehended?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If the Apostle is not perfect, am I perfect?”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And the number of my days, what it is.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I ask of “the number of my days, what it is.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I can speak of “number” without number, and understand “number without number,” in the same sense as “years without years” may be spoken of.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For where there are years, there is a sort of “number” at all events, also.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But yet, “Thou art the same, and Thy years shall not fail.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Make me to know the number of my days;” but “to know what it is.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What then? that number in which thou art, think you that it “is” not?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Assuredly, if I weigh the matter well, it has no being; if I linger behind, it has a sort of being; if I rise above it, it has none.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If, shaking off the trammels of these things, I contemplate things above, if I compare things that pass away with those that endure, I see what has a true being, and what rather seems to be, than really is.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Should I say that these days of mine “are;” and shall I rashly apply this word so full of meaning to this course of things passing away?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To such a degree have I my own self almost ceased to “be, failing” as I am in my weakness, that He escaped from my memory, who said, “I AM HE THAT IS.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hath then any number of days any existence?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In truth it hath, and it is “number without end.”…Everything is swept on by a series of moments, fleeting by, one after the other; there is a torrent of existences ever flowing on and on; a “torrent,” of which He “drank in the way,” who hath now “lift up His Head.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These days then have no true being; they are gone almost before they arrive; and when they are come, they cannot continue; they press upon one another, they follow the one the other, and cannot check themselves in their course.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Of the past nothing is called back again; what is yet to be, is expected as something to pass away again: it is not as yet possessed, whilst as yet it is not arrived; it cannot be kept when once it has arrived.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He asks then concerning “the number of his days, which is;” not that which is “not:” and (which confounds me by a still greater and more perplexing difficulty) at once “is,” and “is not.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We can neither say that “is,” which does not continue; nor that it “is not,” when it has come and is passing.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is that absolute “IS,” that true “IS,” that “IS” in the true sense of the word, that I long for; that “IS;” which “is” in that “Jerusalem” which is “the Bride” of my Lord; where there will not be death, there will not be failing; there will be a day that passeth not away, but continueth: which has neither a yesterday to precede it, nor a to-morrow pressing close upon it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This “number of my days, which is,” this (I say), “make Thou me to know.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “That I may know what is wanting to me.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For while I am struggling here, “this” is wanting unto me: and so long as it is wanting unto me, I do not call myself perfect.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: So long as I have not received it, I say, “not that I have already attained, either am already perfect; but I am pressing towards the prize of God’s high calling.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This let me receive as the prize of my running the race!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There will be a certain resting-place, to terminate my course; and in that resting-place there will be a Country, and no pilgrimage, no dissension, no temptation.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Make me then to know “this number of my days, which is, that I may know what is wanting unto me;” because I am not there yet; lest I should be made proud of what I already am, that “I may be found in Him, not having mine own righteousness.” …

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Behold, thou hast made my days old” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For these days are “waxing old.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I long for new days “that never shall wax old,” that I may say, “Old things have passed away; behold, things are become new.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Already new in hope; then in reality.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For though, in hope and in faith, made new already, how much do we even now do after our old nature!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For we are not so completely “clothed upon” with Christ, as not to bear about with us anything derived from Adam.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Observe that Adam is “waxing old” within us, and Christ is being “renewed” in us.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Though our outward man is perishing, yet is our inward man being renewed day by day.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore, while we fix our thoughts on sin, on mortality, on time, that is hastening by, on sorrow, and toil, and labour, on stages of life following each other in succession, and continuing not, passing on insensibly from infancy even to old age; whilst, I say, we fix our eyes on these things, let us see here “the old man,” the “day that is waxing old;” the Song that is out of date; the Old Testament; when however we turn to the inner man, to those things that are to be renewed in place of these which are to be changed, let us find the “new man,” the “new day,” the “new song,” the “New Testament;” and that “newness,” let us so love, as to have no fears of its “waxing old.”…This man, therefore, who is hasting forward to those things which are new, and “reaching forward to those things which are before,” says, “Lord, make me to know mine end, and the number of my days, which really is, that I may know what is wanting unto me.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: See he still drags with him Adam; and even so he is hasting unto Christ.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Behold,” saith he, “thou hast made my days old.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is those days that are derived from Adam, those days, I say, that thou hast made old.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: They are waxing old day by day: and so waxing old, as to be at some day or other consumed also.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And my substance is as nothing before Thee.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Before Thee, O Lord, my substance is as nothing.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Before Thee;” who seest this; and I too, when I see it, see it only when “before Thee.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When “before men” I see it not.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what shall I say?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What words shall I use to show, that which I now am is nothing in comparison of That which truly “IS”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But it is within that it is said; it is within that it is felt, so far as it is felt.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Before Thee, O Lord,” where Thine eyes are; and not where the eyes of men are.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And where Thine eyes are, what is the state of things?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “That which I am is as nothing.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But, verily, every man living is altogether vanity.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But, verily.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what was he saying above?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Behold, I have already “leaped beyond” all mortal things, and despised things below, have trampled under foot the things of earth, have soared upwards to the delights of the law of the Lord, I have been afloat in the dispensation of the Lord, have yearned for that “End” which Itself is to know no end, have yearned for the number of my days that truly “is,” because the number of days like these hath no real being.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Behold, I am already such a one as this; I have already overleaped so much; I am longing for those things which abide.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But verily,” in the state in which I am here, so long as I am here, so long as I am in this world, so long as I bear mortal flesh, so long as the life of man on earth is a trial, so long as I sigh among causes of offence, as long as while I “stand” I am in “fear lest I fall,” as long as both my good and my ill hangs in uncertainty, “every man living is altogether vanity.”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Albeit man walketh in the Image” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In what “Image,” save that of Him who said, “Let Us make man in Our Image, after Our Likeness.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Albeit man walks in the Image.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the reason he says “albeit,” is, that this is some great thing.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And this “albeit” is followed by “nevertheless,” that the “albeit” which you have already heard, should relate to what is beyond the sun; but this “nevertheless,” which is to follow, to what is “under the sun,” and that the one should relate to the Truth, the other to “vanity.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Albeit,” then, “that man walketh in the Image, nevertheless he is disquieted in vain.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hear the cause of his “disquieting,” and see if it be not a vain one; that thou mayest trample it under foot, that thou mayest “leap beyond it,” and mayest dwell on high, where that “vanity” is not.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What “vanity” is that?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He heapeth up riches, and knoweth not for whom he may be gathering them together.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: O infatuated vanity!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Blessed is the man that maketh the Lord his trust, and hath not respected vanities, nor lying deceits.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To you indeed, O covetous man, to you I seem to be out of my senses, these words appear to you to be “old wives’ tales.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For you, a man of great judgment, and of great prudence, to be sure, are daily devising methods of acquiring money, by traffic, by agriculture, by eloquence perhaps, by making yourself learned in the law, by warfare, perhaps you even add that of usury.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Like a shrewd man as you are, you leave nothing untried, whereby you may pile coin on coin; and may store it up more carefully in a place of secrecy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: You plunder others; you guard against the plunderer; you are afraid lest you should yourself suffer the wrong, that you yourself do; and even what you do suffer, does not correct you.…Examine your own heart, and that prudence of yours, which leads you to deride me, to think me out of my senses for saying these things: and tell me now, “You are heaping up treasures; for whom are you gathering them together?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I see what you would tell me; as if what you would say had not occurred to the person described here; you will say, I am keeping them for my children?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is the voice of parental affection; the excuse of injustice.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I am keeping them” (you say) “for my children.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: So then you are keeping them for your children, are you?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Did not Idithun then know this?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Assuredly he did; but he reckoned it one of the things of the “old days,” that have waxed old, and therefore he despised it: because he was hastening on to the new “days.”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For He, “by whom all things were made,” hath built “mansions” for all of us: thither He would have that which we have go before us; that we may not lose it on earth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When, however, you have kept them on earth, tell me for whom you are to “gather them together”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: You have children: add one more to their number; and give something to Christ also.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He is disquieted in vain.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And now” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And now,” saith this Idithun,—looking back on a certain “vain” show, and looking up to a certain Truth, standing midway where he has something beyond him, and something also behind him, having below him the place from which he took his spring, having above him that toward which he has stretched forth;—“And now,” when I have “over-leaped” some things, when I have trampled many things under foot, when I am no longer captivated by things temporal; even now, I am not perfect, “I have not yet apprehended.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For it is by hope that we are saved; but hope that is seen is not hope; for what a man seeth, why doth he yet hope for?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if we hope for that we see not, then do we with patience wait for it.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore he says: “And now what wait I for?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is it not for the Lord?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He is my expectation, who hath given me all those things, that I might despise them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He will give unto me Himself also, even He who is above all, and “by whom all things were made,” and by whom I was made amongst all; even He, the Lord, is my Expectation!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: You see Idithun, brethren, you see in what way he waiteth for Him!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let no man therefore call himself perfect here; he deceives and imposes upon himself; he is beguiling himself, he cannot have perfection here, and what avails it that he should lose humility?…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And my substance is ever before Thee.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Already advancing, already tending towards Him, and to some extent already beginning to “be,” still (he says) “my substance is ever before Thee.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now that other substance is also before men.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: You have gold, silver, slaves, estates, trees, cattle, servants.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These things are visible even to men.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There is a certain “substance that is ever before Thee.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Deliver me from all my transgressions” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I have “over-leaped” a great deal of ground, a very great deal of ground already; but, “If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the Truth is not in us.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I have “over-leaped” a great deal: but still do I “beat my breast,” and say, “Forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thou therefore art “my expectation!” my “End.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For “Christ is the end of the Law unto righteousness, unto every man that believeth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: From all mine offences: not only from those, that I may not relapse into those which I have already “over-leaped;” but from all, without exception, of those on account of which I now beat my breast, and say, “Forgive us our debts.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Deliver me from all mine offences:” me being thus minded, and holding fast what the Apostle said, “As many of us as be perfect, let us be thus minded.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For at the time that he said that he was not “already perfect,” he then immediately goes on and says, “As many of us as be perfect, let us be thus minded.”…Art thou then, O Apostle, not perfect, and are we perfect?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But hath it escaped you, that he did just now call himself “perfect”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he does not say, “As many of you as are perfect, be ye thus minded;” but “As many of us as be perfect, let us be thus minded;” after having said a little before, “Not that I have already attained; either am already perfect.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In no other way then can you be perfect in this life, than by knowing that you cannot be perfect in this life.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This then will be your perfection, so to have “over-leaped” some things, as to have still some point to which you are hastening on: so as to have something remaining, to which you will have to leap on, when everything else has been passed by.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is such faith as this that is secure; for whoever thinks that he has already attained, is “exalting himself,” so as to be “abased” hereafter.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Thou hast made me the reproach of the foolish.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thou hast so willed it, that I should live among those, and preach the Truth among those, who love vanity; and I cannot but be a laughing-stock to them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For we have been made a spectacle unto this world, and unto angels, and unto men:” to angels who praise, to men who censure, us; or rather to angels, some of whom praise, some of whom are censuring us: and to men also, some of whom are praising, and some censuring us.…Both the one and the other are arms to us: the one “on the right hand,” the other “on the left:” arms however they are both of them; both of these kinds of arms, both those “on the right hand,” and those “on the left;” both those who praise, and those who censure; both those who pay us honour, and those who heap dishonour upon us; with both these kinds I contend against the devil; with both of these I smite him; I defeat him with prosperity, if I be not corrupted by it; by adversity, if I am not broken in spirit by it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I became dumb; and I opened not my mouth” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But it was to guard against “the foolish man,” that “I became dumb, and opened not my mouth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For to whom should I tell what is going on within me?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For I will hear what the Lord God will speak in me; for He will speak peace unto His people.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But “There is no peace,” saith the Lord, “to the wicked.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I was dumb, and opened not my mouth; because it is Thou that madest me.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Was this the reason that thou openedst not thy mouth, “because God made thee”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That is strange; for did not God make thy mouth, that thou shouldest speak?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He that planted the ear, doth He not hear?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He that formed the eye, doth He not see?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: God hath given thee a mouth to speak with; and dost thou say, “I was dumb, and opened not my mouth, because Thou madest me”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or does the clause, “Because Thou madest me,” belong to the verse that follows?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Remove Thy stroke away from me” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Because it is “Thou that hast made me,” let it not be Thy pleasure to destroy me utterly; scourge, so that I may be made better, not so that I faint; beat me, so that I may be beaten out to a greater length and breadth, not so that I may be ground to powder.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “By the heaviness of Thy hand I fainted in corrections.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That is, I “fainted” while Thou wast correcting me.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And what is meant by “correcting” me? except what follows.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Thou with rebukes hast chastened man for iniquity; Thou hast made my life to consume away like a spider” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There is much that is discerned by this Idithun; by every one who discerns as he does; who overleaps as he does.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he says, that he has fainted in God’s corrections; and would fain have the stroke removed away from him, “because it is He who made him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let Him renew me, who also made me; let Him who created me, create me anew.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But yet, Brethren, do we suppose that there was no cause for his fainting, so that he wishes to be “renewed,” to be “created anew”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is “for iniquity,” saith he, “that Thou hast chastened man.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: All this, my having fainted, my being weak, my “crying out of the deep,” all of this is because of “iniquity;” and in this Thou hast not condemned, but hast “chastened” me.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Thou hast chastened man for sin.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hear this more plainly from another Psalm: “It is good for me that Thou hast afflicted me, that I might learn Thy righteousness.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I have been “afflicted,” and at the same time “it is good for me;” it is at once a punishment, and an act of favour.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What hath He in store for us after punishment is over, who inflicts punishment itself by way of favour?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For He it is of whom it was said, “I was brought low, and He made me whole:” and, “It is good for me that Thou hast afflicted me, that I might learn Thy righteousness.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Thou chastenest man for iniquity.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And that which is written, “Thou formest my grief in teaching me,” could only be said unto God by one who was “leaping beyond” his fellows; “Thou formest my grief in teaching me;” Thou makest, that is to say, a lesson for me out of my sorrow.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is Thou that formest that very grief itself; Thou dost not leave it unformed, but formest it; and that grief, that has been inflicted by Thee, when formed, will be a lesson unto me, that I may be set free by Thee.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the word finges is used in the sense of “forming,” as it were moulding, my grief; not in the sense of “feigning” it; in the same way that fingit is applied to the artist, in the same sense that figulus is derived from fingere.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thou therefore “hast chastened man for iniquity.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I see myself in afflictions; I see myself under punishment; and I see no unrighteousness in Thee.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If I therefore am under punishment, and if there is no unrighteousness with Thee, it remains that Thou must have been “chastening man for iniquity.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And by what means hast Thou “chastened” him?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Tell us, O Idithun, the manner of thy chastening; tell us in what way thou hast been “chastened.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And Thou hast made my life consume like a spider.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is the chastening!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What consumes away sooner than the spider?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I speak of the creature itself; though what can be more liable to “consume away” than the spider’s webs?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Observe too how liable to decay is the creature itself.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Do but set your finger lightly upon it, and it is a ruin: there is nothing at all more easily destroyed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To such a state hast Thou brought my life, by chastening me “because of iniquity.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When chastening makes us weak, there is a kind of strength that would be a fault.…It was by a kind of strength that man offended, so as to require to be corrected by weakness: for it was by a certain “pride” that he offended; so as to require to be chastened by humility.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: All proud persons call themselves strong men.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore have many “come from the East and the West,” and have attained “to sit down with Abraham, and Isaac, and Jacob, in the kingdom of Heaven.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore was it that they so attained?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Because they would not be strong.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What is meant by “would not be strong”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: They were afraid to presume of their own merits.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: They did not “go about to establish their own righteousness,” that they might “submit themselves to the righteousness of God.”…Behold! you are mortal; and you bear about you a body of flesh that is corrupting away: “And ye shall fall like one of the princes.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Ye shall die like men,” and shall fall like the devil.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What good does the remedial discipline of mortality do you?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The devil is proud, as not having a mortal body, as being an angel.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But as for you, who have received a mortal body, and to whom even this does no good, so as to humble you by so great weakness, you shall “fall like one of the princes.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This then is the first grace of God’s gift, to bring us to the confession of our infirmity, that whatever good we can do, whatever ability we have, we may be that in Him; that “He that glorieth, may glory in the Lord.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “When I am weak,” saith he, “then am I strong.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But surely every man living disquieteth himself in vain.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He returns to what he mentioned a little before.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Although he be improving here, yet for all that, “every man living disquieteth himself in vain;” forasmuch as he lives in a state of uncertainty.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For who has any assurance even of his own goodness?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He is disquieted in vain.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let him “cast upon the Lord the burden” of his care; let him cast upon Him whatever causes him anxiety.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let Him sustain thee;” let Him keep thee.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For on this earth what is there that is certain, except death?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Consider the whole sum of all the good or the ill of this life, either those belonging to righteousness, or those belonging to unrighteousness; what is there that is certain here, except death?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Have you been advancing in goodness?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: You know what you are to-day; what you will be to-morrow, you know not!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Are you a sinner? you know what you are to-day; what you will be to-morrow, you know not!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: You hope for wealth; it is uncertain whether it will fall to your lot.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: You hope to have a wife; it is uncertain whether you will obtain one, or what sort of one you will obtain.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: You hope for sons: it is uncertain whether they will be born to you.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Are they born? it is uncertain whether they will live: if they live, it is uncertain whether they will grow up in virtue, or whether they will fall away.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whichever way you turn, all is uncertain, death alone is certain.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Art thou poor?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is uncertain whether thou wilt grow rich.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Art thou unlearned?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is uncertain whether thou wilt become learned.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Art thou in feeble health, it is uncertain whether thou wilt regain thy strength.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Art thou born?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is certain that thou wilt die: and in this certainty of death itself, the day of thy death is uncertain.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Amidst these uncertainties, where death alone is certain, while even of that the hour is uncertain, and while it alone is studiously guarded against, though at the same time it is in no way to be escaped, “every man living disquieteth himself in vain.”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Hear my prayer, O Lord” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whereof shall I rejoice?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whereof should I groan?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I rejoice on account of what is past, I groan longing for these which are not yet come.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Hear my prayer, and give ear unto my cry.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hold not Thy peace at my tears.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For do I now no longer weep, because I have already “passed by,” have “left behind” so great things as these?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Do I not weep much the more?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For, “He that increaseth knowledge, increaseth sorrow.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The more I long for what is not here, do I not so much the more groan for it until it comes? do I not so much the more weep until it comes?…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For I am a sojourner with Thee.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But with whom am I a “sojourner”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When I was with the devil, I was a “sojourner;” but then I had a bad host and entertainer; now, however, I am with Thee; but I am a “sojourner” still.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What is meant by a sojourner?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I am a “sojourner” in the place from which I am to remove; not in the place where I am to dwell for ever.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The place where I am to abide for ever, should be rather called my home.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In the place from which I am to remove I am a “sojourner;” but yet it is with my God that I am a sojourner, with whom I am hereafter to abide, when I have reached my home.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what home is that to which you are to remove from this estate of a sojourner?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Recognise that home, of which the Apostle speaks, “We have an habitation of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the Heavens.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If this house is eternal in the Heavens, when we have come to it, we shall not be sojourners any more.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For how should you be a sojourner in an eternal home?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But here, where the Master of the house is some day to say to you, “Remove,” while you yourself know not when He will say it, be thou in readiness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And by longing for your eternal home, you will be keeping yourself in readiness for it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And be not angry with Him, because He gives thee notice to remove, when He Himself pleases.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For He made no covenant with thee, nor did He bind Himself by any engagement; nor didst thou enter upon the tenancy of this house on a certain stipulation for a definite term: thou art to quit, when it is its Master’s pleasure.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For therefore is it that you now dwell there free of charge.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For I am a sojourner with Thee, and a stranger.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore it is there is my country: it is there is my home.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I am a sojourner with Thee, and a stranger.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Here too is understood “with Thee.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For many are strangers with the devil: but they who have already believed and are faithful, are, it is true, “strangers” as yet, because they have not yet come to that country and to that home: but still they are strangers with God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For so long as we are in the body, we are strangers from the Lord, and we desire, whether we are strangers, or abiding here, “we may be accepted with Him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I am a “sojourner with Thee; and a stranger, as all my fathers were.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If then I am as all my fathers were, shall I say that I will not remove, when they have removed?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Am I to lodge here on other terms, than those on which they lodged here also?…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Grant me some remission, that I may be refreshed before I go hence” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Consider well, Idithun, consider what knots those are which thou wouldest have “loosed” unto thee, that thou mightest be “refreshed before thou goest hence.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For thou hast certain fever-heats from which thou wouldest fain be refreshed, and thou sayest, “that I may be refreshed,” and “grant me a remission.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What should He remit, or loosen unto thee, save that difficulty under which, and in consequence of which, thou sayest, “Forgive us our debts.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Grant me a remission before I go hence, and be no more.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Set me free from my sins, “before I go hence,” that I may not go hence with my sins.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Remit them unto me, that I may be set at rest in my conscience, that it may be disburthened of its feverish anxiety, the anxiety with which “I am sorry for my sin.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Grant me a remission, that I may be refreshed” (before everything else), “before I go hence, and be no more.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For if thou grantest me not a “remission, that I may be refreshed,” I shall “go and be no more.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Before I go” thither, where if I go, I shall thenceforth “be no more.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Grant me a remission, that I may be refreshed.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: A question has suggested itself, how he will be no more.…What is meant then by “shall be no more,” unless Idithun is alluding to what is true “being,” and what is not true “being.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he was beholding with the mind, with which he could do so, with the “mind’s eye,” by which he was able to behold it, that end, which he had desired to have shown unto him, saying, “Lord, make me to know mine end.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He was beholding “the number of his days, which truly is;” and he observed that all that is below, in comparison of that true being, has no true being.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For those things are permanent; these are subject to change; mortal, and frail, and the eternal suffering, though full of corruption, is for this very reason not to be ended, that it may ever be being ended without end.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He alluded therefore to that realm of bliss, to the happy country, to the happy home, where the Saints are partakers of eternal Life, and of Truth unchangeable; and he feared to “go” where that is not, where there is no true being; longing to be there, where “Being” in the highest sense is!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is on account of this contrast then, while standing midway between them, he says, “Grant me a remission, that I may be refreshed before I go hence and be no more.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For if Thou “grantest me not a remission” of my sins, I shall go from Thee unto all eternity!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And from whom shall I go to all eternity?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: From Him who said, I Am HE that Am: from Him who said, “Say unto the children of Israel, I Am hath sent me unto you.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He then who goes from Him, in the contrary direction, goes to non-existence.…
